skill.name: mcp-transport-guide
skill.description: Guides developers through MCP transport choices, including stdio, SSE, HTTP streaming, custom transports, deployment, testing, and security tradeoffs.
skill.summary: Choose and implement MCP transports for local, cloud, and web deployments.
skill.author: EmilLindfors

security_audit.summary: Static command, network, sensitive-file, weak-crypto, and heuristic detections were reviewed as documentation examples rather than executable skill behavior. No prompt injection or malicious intent was found. The skill is publishable with a medium warning because several examples use broad network binding, permissive CORS, and full request logging patterns that can be unsafe if copied without hardening.

medium_findings.title: Broad network listener examples may expose MCP services
medium_findings.description: Several remote transport examples bind to 0.0.0.0:3000. This is legitimate for tutorials, but copied code can expose an MCP server on every interface unless paired with firewall rules, authentication, and TLS.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code is clearly tutorial content, not hidden execution. The risk is real if users copy the listener examples into production without access controls.

medium_findings.title: Permissive CORS example allows any origin
medium_findings.description: The CORS security section uses allow_origin(Any) while allowing authorization and content-type headers. This is convenient for examples but can be unsafe for authenticated remote MCP services.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The permissive CORS policy is explicit in the security section. It may be acceptable for local testing, but production guidance should restrict trusted origins.

medium_findings.title: Request logging example may expose sensitive payloads
medium_findings.description: The observability example logs the full request object. MCP requests may contain prompts, tool arguments, or authorization metadata, so production logging should redact sensitive fields.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The example directly logs a request value. The impact depends on request contents and logging sinks, but sensitive data exposure is a realistic operational risk.

low_findings.title: Static command execution detections are fenced examples
low_findings.description: The external command and Ruby backtick detections occur in Markdown code fences or Rust async task examples. The skill contains no executable script files, install hooks, or hidden command runners.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line-number review shows these detections are tutorial snippets in SKILL.md. The tokio::spawn hits are asynchronous tasks, not operating-system process spawning.

low_findings.title: Static network detections are transport examples
low_findings.description: The network findings are expected in a guide about MCP stdio, SSE, HTTP, and WebSocket transports. They document local servers and tests rather than making outbound requests from the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced lines are Rust examples, URLs, and test calls in documentation. No evidence found of data exfiltration or runtime network activity by the skill itself.

low_findings.title: Certificate and weak-crypto detections are false positives
low_findings.description: The certificate/key file reference is a TLS configuration example. The weak-cryptography detections are keyword collisions in descriptive MCP transport text and do not show insecure crypto implementation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The relevant lines contain descriptions or TLS placeholder file names, not cryptographic code. No evidence found of weak algorithm use.

dangerous_patterns.title: Documentation combines network servers and operational commands
dangerous_patterns.description: The critical heuristic is a false positive for malicious behavior, but the documentation does combine network listener examples, Docker commands, and deployment snippets. Users should treat these as templates that require environment-specific hardening.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The combination is present, but it is transparent educational content. There is no obfuscation, credential access, or hidden execution path.

content.value_statement: MCP servers need the right transport for local tools, web services, and cloud deployments. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code compare stdio, SSE, HTTP streaming, and custom options with security tradeoffs.
actual_capabilities: Explains when to use stdio, SSE, HTTP streaming, or custom MCP transports.
Provides Rust-oriented examples for transport setup and service wiring.
Compares transports by deployment scenario, latency, throughput, scalability, and complexity.
Covers authentication, TLS, rate limiting, CORS, logging, metrics, and tracing topics.
Shows testing and deployment patterns for local binaries, Docker, and Kubernetes.
limitations: Does not provide a complete production-ready MCP server implementation.
Examples require adaptation to current rmcp, axum, and deployment versions.
Security examples are templates and do not replace a full threat model.
Focuses on Rust examples and may need translation for other languages.

use_cases.title: Select a transport for a new MCP server
use_cases.description: Compare stdio, SSE, HTTP streaming, and custom transports against deployment needs and client access patterns.

use_cases.title: Review remote MCP deployment plans
use_cases.description: Check whether a cloud or web MCP service includes TLS, authentication, rate limiting, CORS controls, and monitoring.

use_cases.title: Improve transport test coverage
use_cases.description: Plan unit, integration, and load tests for stdio, HTTP, and streaming transport behavior.

prompt_templates.title: Pick a Transport
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me choose an MCP transport for a server that runs in [environment] and is used by [client type]. Explain the tradeoffs.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Local Server
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a stdio MCP transport setup for a local Rust server. Include logging rules, error handling, and testing steps.

prompt_templates.title: Harden a Remote Transport
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my planned SSE or HTTP MCP transport. Identify authentication, TLS, CORS, rate limiting, and logging risks.

prompt_templates.title: Plan Custom Transport Architecture
prompt_templates.prompt: Compare a custom MCP transport against HTTP streaming for [constraints]. Include reliability, backpressure, observability, and deployment risks.

output_examples.input: I need an MCP server for Claude Desktop on one machine.
output_examples.output: Recommended transport: stdio. It fits local subprocess communication, avoids remote network exposure, and keeps logging on stderr.

output_examples.input: I need a cloud-hosted MCP service behind a load balancer.
output: Required controls: TLS, bearer token validation, rate limiting, restricted CORS, and redacted request logs.

output_examples.input: I need real-time server updates for browser-connected clients.
output_examples.output: SSE can fit server-pushed updates, but authentication, origin restrictions, timeout handling, and reconnection behavior need careful design.

best_practices: Start transport selection from deployment location, client type, and security requirements.
Use TLS, authentication, rate limiting, and restricted CORS for every remote transport.
Test the transport independently before testing business logic on top of it.
anti_patterns: Do not expose a remote MCP server on all interfaces without access controls.
Do not log full request objects that may contain prompts, tokens, or tool arguments.
Do not choose a custom transport before validating standard stdio, SSE, and HTTP options.

faq.question: Which MCP transport is best for Claude Desktop?
faq.answer: stdio is usually the best fit because Claude Desktop starts and communicates with local MCP servers as subprocesses.

faq.question: When should I use HTTP streaming?
faq.answer: Use HTTP streaming for web services, API gateways, load balancers, and environments that need standard HTTP infrastructure.

faq.question: When should I use SSE?
faq.answer: Use SSE when the server needs to push updates to clients and your infrastructure supports long-lived HTTP connections.

faq.question: Does this skill create an MCP server for me?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance and examples that you adapt to your own service code and deployment environment.

faq.question: Are the examples production ready?
faq.answer: No. Treat them as starting points and add environment-specific authentication, TLS, CORS limits, logging controls, and monitoring.

faq.question: Can this help with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code workflows?
faq.answer: Yes. It gives prompts and transport guidance that can support MCP work across Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
